preFix.title: Fitting a thermostatic radiator valve
preFix.subtitle: Replace a manual radiator valve with a thermostatic valve head and body on an existing radiator
preFix.excerpt: How to replace a manual radiator valve with a thermostatic radiator valve (TRV), covering draining the affected radiator, removing the old valve, fitting the new body and head, and refilling the system.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Heating and Cooling of Buildings (public domain engineering references).

content.text: A
TRV

replaces the manual hand-wheel valve on the flow side of a radiator. Fitting a TRV involves draining the radiator (not the whole system), removing the old valve body, fitting the new one, and refilling. The
lockshield valve
on the return side is closed during the work and returned to its original setting after.
What you will need: materials

The new TRV: specify the connection size (15 mm is standard) and the orientation (angled, straight, or corner-type) to match the existing pipe run. The TRV body fits on the flow side (the pipe that carries hot water into the radiator); the lockshield valve stays on the return. Check the existing valve body threads: most UK valves are 15 mm compression or 1/2 inch BSP. A replacement TRV must match. Bring two old towels and the bucket to catch any water left in the radiator.
Step 1, note the lockshield position and close both valves

Turn off the heating system. Remove the plastic cap from the lockshield valve and count the number of turns it takes to close it fully, noting this for refitting. Close the manual valve on the flow side (turn clockwise until it stops). Close the lockshield valve on the return side. Both valves must be fully closed before disconnecting any union.
Step 2, drain the radiator

Place the bucket below the flow valve (flow is usually the valve at the top-left or bottom-left of the radiator, with the hot supply pipe). Undo the union nut that connects the valve body to the radiator with the adjustable spanner, holding the valve body steady with the water-pump pliers. Expect 1 to 2 litres of water to drain from the radiator. Insert a radiator bleed key into the bleed valve at the top of the radiator to allow air in and speed draining.
Step 3, remove the old valve body

With the union nut free from the radiator, undo the cap nut at the pipe end to release the valve body from the supply pipe. The cap nut has an olive or compression ring inside it; the olive may be stuck to the pipe. If the olive is stuck, the pipe must be cut back 30 mm and a new olive fitted on the clean pipe end. Clean the pipe end of any old PTFE compound before fitting the new valve.
Step 4, fit the new TRV body

Wrap PTFE tape three times clockwise around any threaded connections on the new valve body. Slide the cap nut over the pipe, insert the new olive, and connect the valve body to the pipe. Hand-tighten the cap nut and give one and a quarter turns with the spanner. Connect the union nut from the valve body to the radiator valve tail, with the fibre or rubber washer in place. Hand-tighten, then one full turn with the spanner.
Step 5, refill and fit the TRV head

Open the lockshield valve the same number of turns as noted before. Open the new TRV body to the fully-open position. Allow the radiator to fill: water will push up from the flow side. Close the bleed valve as soon as water begins to dribble from it, then open it briefly to release trapped air. Check all connections for drips and tighten any that seep. Screw or clip the TRV head onto the valve body: most heads are self-setting once pressed on. Set the head to the required temperature setting.
Variations

A smart TRV head replaces the standard wax head with a motor-driven version that connects to a heating control app. The valve body fitting is the same; only the head differs. On a heat pump system, set all TRV heads to the maximum position: the heat pump controls flow temperature directly and TRV throttling forces it to raise the flow temperature, reducing efficiency.
Care

At the end of the heating season, check that the TRV head pin moves freely: the pin sticks to the valve seat in summer when it has not moved for months, and the TRV will not open when heating starts in autumn. Remove the head and press the pin several times by hand to free it. Dosing the system water with a central-heating inhibitor (check and top up annually) prevents the system corrosion that causes TRV pins to seize.

attrs.fix: Remove the TRV head and check that the pin in the valve body moves freely when pressed. If stuck, spray the pin with a small amount of WD-40 and work it up and down. Replace the head once the pin moves freely.
attrs.cause: The TRV head pin is stuck against the valve seat; the valve body is not opening.
attrs.symptom: Radiator stays cold after the new TRV is fitted

attrs.fix: Turn off both valves, put the bucket in place, undo the union nut, check the washer is in the recess and not folded over, and reassemble. Give one full turn of the spanner after hand-tight.
attrs.cause: The fibre washer was not seated correctly, or the union nut was not tightened enough.
attrs.symptom: Water drips from the union nut joint after refilling

attrs.fix: Dry the pipework and connections thoroughly and wipe with tissue paper to find the source. Run the system for 30 minutes and check again. Any connection that is wet needs a further quarter-turn to close.
attrs.cause: A small drip at the union or cap nut joint is running down the pipe and evaporating before it forms a visible puddle.
attrs.symptom: System pressure drops after refitting the TRV
